coredata.dc:title: Lignin-derived carbon dots with molten salt confinement for ultra-stable room-temperature phosphorescence

coredata.dc:description: In response to the critical challenges of high toxicity, complex synthesis, and poor environmental stability in conventional room-temperature phosphorescent materials, this study presents a sustainable biomass-derived strategy through the development of lignin-based carbon dot/inorganic salt composites (CDSLX-T). By employing a one-step molten salt approach, we construct a hierarchical architecture featuring lignin-derived sp2-hybridized carbon cores and a rigid crystalline shell comprising multi-component inorganic salts (MgO/Mg3(PO4)2/KCl/KNO3). The inherent conjugated moieties of lignin facilitate efficient intersystem crossing, while nitrogen doping optimizes (n, π*) electronic configurations to enhance spin-orbit coupling effects. In situ characterization elucidates the synergistic mechanism of high-temperature-induced carbon core aromatization and salt matrix crystallization. A dual confinement strategy-combining covalent bonding (C-K/C-Cl/C-Mg) and spatial restriction-effectively suppresses carbon dot vibrations and non-radiative triplet exciton transitions, enabling stable phosphorescence emission in aqueous environments. Leveraging time-resolved luminescence characteristics with tunable lifetimes, the material demonstrates unique potential in fingerprint visualization and multilevel information encryption. This work establishes an eco-friendly synthetic paradigm for high-performance bio-based optical materials, bridging sustainable chemistry with advanced photonic applications.

head.ce:title: Lignin-derived carbon dots with molten salt confinement for ultra-stable room-temperature phosphorescence

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In response to the critical challenges of high toxicity, complex synthesis, and poor environmental stability in conventional room-temperature phosphorescent materials, this study presents a sustainable biomass-derived strategy through the development of lignin-based carbon dot/inorganic salt composites (CDSLX-T). By employing a one-step molten salt approach, we construct a hierarchical architecture featuring lignin-derived sp2-hybridized carbon cores and a rigid crystalline shell comprising multi-component inorganic salts (MgO/Mg3(PO4)2/KCl/KNO3). The inherent conjugated moieties of lignin facilitate efficient intersystem crossing, while nitrogen doping optimizes (n, π*) electronic configurations to enhance spin-orbit coupling effects. In situ characterization elucidates the synergistic mechanism of high-temperature-induced carbon core aromatization and salt matrix crystallization. A dual confinement strategy-combining covalent bonding (C-K/C-Cl/C-Mg) and spatial restriction-effectively suppresses carbon dot vibrations and non-radiative triplet exciton transitions, enabling stable phosphorescence emission in aqueous environments. Leveraging time-resolved luminescence characteristics with tunable lifetimes, the material demonstrates unique potential in fingerprint visualization and multilevel information encryption. This work establishes an eco-friendly synthetic paradigm for high-performance bio-based optical materials, bridging sustainable chemistry with advanced photonic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials exhibit persistent afterglow through efficient triplet exciton storage mechanisms, enabling delayed photon release for seconds to hours post-excitation [1]. These systems surpass conventional fluorescent materials in three critical metrics: microsecond-to-second scale lifetimes, environmental stimulus responsiveness, and background-free detection capability [2]. While current RTP platforms predominantly employ organic compounds and organometallic complexes, their practical deployment is hindered by intrinsic limitations: organic systems suffer from compromised stability under ambient conditions, whereas metal-containing counterparts face toxicity constraints [3,4]. The emergence of carbon dots (CDs) as nanoscale carbon-based luminophores has revolutionized RTP material design. Comprising sp2/sp3 hybridized cores with tunable surface functionalities (-NH2, -COOH, -C=O), CDs demonstrated phosphorescent quantum yields up to 23 % when embedded in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrices [5]. This advancement circumvented traditional toxicity and processing challenges, opening new avenues for biocompatible optoelectronics. Nevertheless, the manifestation of RTP CDs demands precise engineering of electronic configurations capable of facilitating efficient intersystem crossing (ISC). While most CDs exhibit fluorescence through π → π* transitions, only a minority achieve RTP due to insufficient spin-orbit coupling and elevated singlet-triplet energy gaps [6]. Conventional strategies employing citric acid or phenolic precursors introduce n → π* transitions to promote triplet excitons generation through carbonyl groups and small aromatic clusters (3–5 rings), yet suffer from inherent drawbacks: electronic homogeneity from monodisperse oxygen functionalities, thermal degradation of active sites, and oxygen quenching [7]. These limitations underscore the critical need for advanced precursor systems with hierarchical conjugation architectures and stabilized triplet exciton channels [8]. The shift toward sustainable carbon dot synthesis has highlighted biomass as an ideal precursor due to its inherent carbon enrichment and pre-organized aromatic frameworks. Lignocellulosic components, with their fused ring structures, facilitate spontaneous hybridization during pyrolysis, forming extended conjugation domains essential for exciton delocalization. These oxygen-rich groups on the carbon material's surface introduce surface defects, offering numerous tunable reactive sites that serve as exciton trapping centers, thereby modulating the CDs' optical emission properties [9]. Additionally, these chemical groups provide carbon dot-based RTP composites with versatile and tunable reaction sites, enhancing their functional potential [10]. Despite their biomimetic advantages, lignin-derived CDs face intrinsic constraints stemming from lignin's structural complexity. Key challenges include low quantum yields (QYs), restricted surface functional diversity, and narrow emission profiles, collectively impairing fluorescence detectability and long-lived phosphorescence [11]. Heteroatom doping has proven to be a powerful strategy for tuning the photoluminescence properties and boosting the QYs of CDs. By modifying the internal electronic structure of the material, this technique increases the presence of (n, π*) configurations in the triplet states' electronic structure. Moreover, it enhances spin-orbit coupling (SOC) between singlet and triplet states, which elevates the ISC rate constant. These changes lead to a higher population of triplet excitons, thereby facilitating phosphorescence emission [12]. When heteroatoms are incorporated into lignin, electron-donating groups (e.g., -OH and -OCH3) and electron-accepting heteroatoms on the aromatic ring create a “D-π-A” structure. This arrangement promotes effective separation between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), narrowing the molecular band gap. As a result, charge transfer efficiency is significantly improved, and the optical lifetime of the synthesized CDs is extended [13].

On the other hand, triplet excitons generated in carbon CDs through ISC are highly susceptible to external environmental factors such as oxygen, water, and pH, which often lead to phosphorescence quenching. To achieve effective phosphorescence emission from CDs, it is crucial to minimize non-radiative transitions and quenching effects [14]. One effective approach involves embedding CDs within a stable and rigid matrix, such as polymers, crystalline materials, mesoporous structures, or inorganic shells. These matrices stabilize triplet states and suppress non-radiative transitions through covalent, hydrogen, or ionic bonding interactions with the CDs [15]. However, conventional methods for preparing phosphorescent composites typically involve a two-step process: synthesizing CDs followed by compositing with the matrix. This approach often requires secondary high-temperature treatments, which can degrade the active functional groups on the CDs' surface, thereby reducing the quantum yield and optical lifetime of the resulting phosphorescent materials. Furthermore, the weak interaction between the CDs and the matrix makes the composites vulnerable to liquid-induced phosphorescence quenching [16]. An alternative and promising strategy is the use of molten salts (MS), which are solid at room temperature but transition to a liquid phase at elevated temperatures. Molten salts offer several advantages, including tunable temperature ranges, high thermal stability, and excellent solubility. These properties facilitate ion migration and diffusion through Coulombic interactions between positively and negatively charged particles, enabling the efficient generation of CDs [17]. Unlike traditional encapsulation methods, the molten salt approach simplifies the process, allows precise temperature control, and minimizes damage to the active sites on the CDs' surface during high-temperature treatment. Additionally, this method can generate uncommon valence and ionic species during the reaction, leading to the formation of novel complex oxides and highly stable rigid salt shells. The in-situ generated CDs are tightly embedded within these shells, which not only promotes the formation of triplet excitons but also stabilizes them, enhancing phosphorescence performance [18].

Herein, his study presents a simple and efficient molten salt synthesis method for producing CD-based room-temperature phosphorescent composites. Sodium lignosulfonate and dopamine hydrochloride serve as carbon and dual carbon/nitrogen sources, respectively, while KNO3, MgCl2, and KH2PO4 act as inorganic molten salts. The effects of reaction temperature and dopamine hydrochloride ratio on the CDs' structural and luminescent properties are systematically analyzed. The formation pathways of the inorganic salt shell, its interaction with the CDs, and its impact on luminescence are also explored. The versatility of this approach is validated using other biomass materials, such as chitosan, microcrystalline cellulose, and black liquor lignin. Leveraging the high phosphorescence visibility and lifetime variations of the composites, potential applications in fingerprint recognition and information encryption are investigated. This work offers new theoretical insights for developing sustainable, biomass-based room-temperature phosphorescent materials.

Sodium Lignosulfonate (SL, C20H24Na2O10S2) was obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Company. Dopamine hydrochloride (C8H11NO2·HCl, 98.0 %) was purchased from Beijing Jinming Biotechnology Company. Potassium nitrate (KNO3, 99.0 %) and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O, 98.0 %) were obtained from the National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Company. Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, 99.5 %) was obtained from Tianjin Dingshengxin Chemical Company. Anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH, 99.7 wt%) was obtained from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Company. Chitosan (degree of deacetylation ≥95 %, Mw ≈ 50,000 Da) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Company. Poplar bark powder was collected from a paper-making factory (Mudanjiang, Heilongjiang, China). P-toluenesulfonic acid (p-TsOH，99.0 %) and microcrystalline cellulose (MCC，99.0 %) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Company.
All the chemicals were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent material CDSLX-T
ce:section.ce:para: SL (0.025 g), dopamine hydrochloride (mass proportions of 0 %, 5 %, 10 %, and 20 %, respectively), KNO3 (1.0 g), MgCl2 (0.35 g) and KH2PO4 (0.05 g) were weighed and dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and stirred to form a homogeneous mixed solution. This solution was frozen and vacuum dried to obtain a yellow powder and then it was transferred to a crucible and heated to different target temperatures (300–450 °C) in a muffle furnace at a rate of 10 °C per minute for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid was ground into powder and named as CDSLX-T, where X denotes the mass ratio of dopamine and T represents the reaction temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of room temperature phosphorescent materials with different carbon sources
ce:section.ce:para: Black liquor lignin was prepared according previous method [19]. Control experiments were conducted using different biomass carbon sources (chitosan, black liquor lignin, and MCC) heated to 360 °C in a muffle furnace while other experimental conditions were kept constant. The obtained phosphorescent materials were named as CDC-360, CDBL-360, and CDM-360, respectively.

The fluorescence and phosphorescence properties of CDSLX-T were obtained with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan), the excitation and emission slit widths were set to 5 nm, and the scanning rate is 1200 nm/min for fluorescence recording, while for phosphorescence recording, the excitation slit width was set to 10 nm with an emission slit width of 20 nm, and the scanning step was 240 nm/min. UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were obtained using a UV spectrophotometer (UV1006M031, VARIAN, USA) with a wavelength scan range of 200–800 nm. In situ and ex situ tests of the crystallinity of the samples were recorded by X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (Rigaku D/Max-Rb, Tokyo, Japan), using Cu-Kα radiation with a scanning range of 10–80°. For in-situ recording, the temperature was controlled by a Paar HKT 1200 system, the temperature was increased from room temperature (25 °C) to 450 °C at a rate of 1 °C min−1, the patterns of the heating process at 25–450 °C and the cooling process at 450–25 °C were recorded. Raman spectra (grating 1800 lines per mm) were obtained by Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, UK) with argon ion laser excitation at 514 nm. The surface morphology and microstructure images of samples were obtained by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Jeol JEM-2011, Japan). The binding energy was evaluated with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI5700, Physical Electronics Inc., Chanhassen, MN, USA) with Al–Kα as the radioactive source (hν = 1486.6 eV) and was determined using the C1s curve at 284.8 eV as a reference standard. Fluorescence time-resolved decay curves, fluorescence quantum yields, and phosphorescence time-resolved decay curves were tested using a fluorescence spectrometer ((FL3C-111TCSPC, HORIBA, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Information encryption and fingerprint detection application
ce:section.ce:para: CDSL10–300, CDSL10–360, and CDSL10–400 powders were used to represent English letters, and the images were taken with a cellular phone under daylight and UV (365 nm) irradiation as well as with the UV light removed. CDSL10–300 and CDSL10–400 powders were used to represent the “888” encrypted message, and images of the letters of the alphabet were taken under UV light (254 nm) irradiation and removal of UV light. Fingerprints were obtained by pressing a finger on the glass surface to obtain latent fingerprints, spreading CDSL10–400 powder evenly on the fingerprint surface and keeping it there for a few seconds, removing the excess powder with a fingerprint brush, irradiating the glass surface with fingerprints under a UV light (254 nm), and photographing the fingerprint images. CDSL10–400 was dissolved in ultrapure water to prepare a 1.0 mg·mL−1 aqueous solution. Letters were drawn by a paintbrush on a glass plate and dried naturally, and then the images of the filter paper under sunlight and UV light (254 nm) were taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical property analysis of CDSLX-T
ce:section.ce:para: CDSLX-T composite phosphorescent materials are prepared in one step by the molten salt method (Scheme 1 ). Carbon source sodium lignosulfonate, nitrogen source hydrochloride dopamine, and molten salts KNO3, MgCl2, and KH2PO4 are mixed, freeze-drying, and then heated at 400 °C for 2 h. During high temperature, inorganic salts becoming liquid accelerate the reaction process, leading to in situ formation of CDs from sodium lignosulfonate, and the molten salts recrystallizing to form a solid salt shell structure upon cooling. The stable benzene ring structure and the abundant oxygen atoms from lignin are favorable for forming carbon cores and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs. The doping of dopamine hydrochloride provides additional CN groups to regulate CDs excitation energy and transition orbital characteristics, while the inorganic salt shell protects the triplet excitons from quenching, making the CDs emit blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation and yellow-green phosphorescence when turned off. Meanwhile, the inorganic protective salt shells prevent CDs from external quenching factors and restrict intramolecular motions, reducing non-radiative decay for a stable phosphorescence emission in liquid.

The effects of dopamine on the phosphorescent properties of the constructed composites are firstly discussed. Under 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. 1a-(a-III)), all CDSLX-400 samples exhibit blue fluorescence, and present yellow-green afterglow phenomenon after turning off the UV irradiation. The afterglow coming from the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs of CDSL-400 is about 5 s, and it increases to 7 s after the addition of dopamine, which is ascribed to the introduction of heteroatom nitrogen that improves the surface defects of the CDs [20]. When the mass ratio of dopamine loading exceeds 10 %, no significant change in the RTP time is observed, thus the optimal doping ratio of dopamine hydrochloride selected is 10 %.

The optical properties of CDSLX-400 are characterized by UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence, and phosphorescence spectrum (Fig. 1 and Table S1). When the excitation wavelength is increased from 320 nm to 420 nm, the emission peak intensities of all CDSLX-400 samples increase first and then decrease, accompanied by a redshift of the peaks, indicating their excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. 1b-(b-III)). The characteristic excitation wavelength for CDSL-400, CDSL5–400, CDSL10–400, and CDSL20–400 is 350 nm, and the corresponding characteristic emission wavelengths are 397, 398, 399, and 403 nm, with fluorescence quantum yields (FLQYs) of 6.89 %, 7.34 %, 7.98 %, and 8.50 %, respectively (Table S1). By introducing heteroatomic nitrogen, the FLQY increases from 6.89 % to 8.50 %, due to the nitrogen-containing functional groups enhance the electron density and raise the Fermi level [21]. The fluorescence spectra of all samples are quite similar, but as the proportion of hydrochloride dopamine increases, the maximum emission peak slightly redshifts. When the mass ratio of dopamine reaches 20 %, the half-peak width of CDs samples significantly increases, indicating the presence of multiple fluorescence emission centers attributed to the increase in nitrogen-containing functional groups that induce the formation of new energy levels. According to the Commission International d'Éclairage (CIE) 1931 chromaticity chart, the fluorescent color coordinates of CDSL10–400 is located at (0.14358, 0.04927), which is in the blue region (Fig. S1). CDSLX-400 has a similar UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1c-(c-III)), indicating a similar structural composition. The two characteristic absorption peaks located at 240 and 310 nm correspond to the π-π* conjugation of aromatic CC and the n-π* transition of C=O/C=N, respectively, suggesting the existence of CC conjugated structures and surface defect states in the CDs. The wavelength dependence of excitation mainly arises from the presence of multiple functional groups on the surface of the CDs, and the abundant surface defect states facilitate ISC. The phosphorescence spectra show (Fig. 1d-(d-III)) that the characteristic excitation wavelengths of CDSLX-400 are 350 nm, and the corresponding characteristic emission wavelengths are at 499 nm, 501 nm, 503 nm, and 505 nm, respectively, with relative fluorescence redshift, which demonstrates excitation wavelength-dependent behavior [22,23]. The fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of CDSLX-400 exhibit excitation wavelength dependence, which may originate from the presence of multiple functional groups (CO, CN, etc.) on the surface of the CDs, resulting in the formation of multiple energy levels within the energy gaps and the corresponding multiple electron transitions, giving rise to excitation-dependent behavior [24]. CDSLX-400 samples exhibit relative fluorescence redshift and a significant stokes shift, and the phosphorescence stokes shift is significantly larger than that of fluorescence. It originates from the existence of triplet states in the phosphorescence emission process, where the energy of the triplet state is lower than that of the singlet state, resulting in a longer emission wavelength for CDSLX-400.

The UV–vis spectra of CDSLX-400 are divided into three absorption bands: Band I comes from typical π-π* transition in the sp2 structural domains of the carbon cores of CDs; Band II is from n-π* transition of double-bonded unsaturated groups containing heteroatoms; and Band III, denotes a lower-energy absorption band attributed to the contribution of the surface defect states of the CDs (Fig. 2a). The absorption bands of CDSLX-400 include the π-π* transitions in band I, the n-π* transitions in band II, and a small amount from band III. CDSL-400 has a large number of π-π* transitions and a certain amount of n-π* transitions originating from aromatic carbon core (C=C) and oxygen-containing functional groups in CDs. The highly stable benzene ring structure in lignin is retained in the carbon core structure, while the introduction of an oxygen atom into the sp2 domain induces the emergence of an n-orbital energy level, resulting in an n-π* transition of electrons and facilitating ISC, producing a sufficient number of triplet excitons [25]. From CDSL-400 to CDSL20–400, as the proportion of dopamine hydrochloride increases, the intensity of the absorption peak corresponding to the n-π* transition at 300 nm enhances. Charge asymmetry and electron density redistribution from heteroatom nitrogen in the CDs result in formation of new n orbital energy levels within the CDs, which gives the CDs different emission centers. The introduction of dopamine hydrochloride effectively promotes the formation of groups such as C=O/C=N, accelerating the ISC process in order to produce a large number of triplet excitons [26]. Therefore, as the proportion of hydrochloric acid dopamine added increases, the phosphorescence emission time gradually increases. The fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of CDSLX-400 are tested (Fig. 2 b-e), and the data are analyzed by fitting eq. 1. Both fluorescence and phosphorescence decay spectrum can be fitted with a triple exponential function [27]:

where τ1, τ2, and τ3 are the three components of the lifetime, and α1, α2, and α3 are the corresponding exponential factors. The fluorescence decay spectrum of the CDSL10–400 can be fitted as 1.11 ns (33 %), 4.88 ns (44 %), and 13.79 ns (23 %), with a fluorescence average lifetime of 10.07 ns. The phosphorescence decay curves can be fitted as 0.67 (55 %), 0.67 (40 %), and 5.95 s (5 %), and the average phosphorescence lifetime is 2.35 s. The fluorescence lifetimes of CDSLx-400 are 8.03 ns, 8.23 ns, 10.07 ns, and 10.08 ns, respectively (Table S2). The N atoms can improve charge delocalization, which is beneficial for suppressing fluorescence decay. Therefore, CDSL20–400 has the longest fluorescence lifetime. After fitting calculations, the CDSLX-400 phosphorescence lifetimes are 2.16 s, 2.34 s, 2.35 s, and 2.34 s, respectively (Table S3), which are much longer than those of the conventional phosphorescent materials reported previously, and also have a significant lifetime advantage over other matrix-composite CDs [28–30]. Compared with CDSL-400, the addition of heteroatomic nitrogen significantly increases the phosphorescence lifetime, which is consistent with the phosphorescence time images. Fig. 2f shows the schematic of the FL and RTP mechanisms of CDSLX-400. The energy gap difference between singlet (S1) and triplet states (Tn) is the energy gap (ΔEST). The smaller the ΔEST, the easier it is for electrons to cross the gap and reach Tn, which is more favorable for phosphorescence emission [13]. The energy gap difference between singlet (S1) and triplet states (Tn) is the energy level gap (ΔEST), which is determined by the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the samples [31]. The ΔEST is calculated as follows:

where h and c are Planck's constant and the speed of light, respectively [32]. The λon-set used in the ES1 calculation is the characteristic fluorescence emission wavelength (λS1) of the samples, and the λon-set used in the ETn calculation is the characteristic phosphorescence emission wavelength (λTn) of the samples under the corresponding characteristic excitation [13]. The ΔEST of CDSLX-400 are calculated to be 0.64 eV, 0.64 eV, 0.63 eV, and 0.62 eV, respectively, according to eq. 2 (Table S4). A smaller ΔEST is favorable to promote the ISC process, which contributes to the generation of triplet excitons. During high-temperature carbonization, the abundant aromatic rings in lignin undergo dehydrogenation and condensation reactions to form highly ordered sp2-hybridized carbon cores, which serve as stable fluorescent centers [33]. Simultaneously, oxygen-containing functional groups partially retained on the CDs' surface form surface defects that act as exciton-trapping centers, enhancing fluorescence emission [34]. With the increase of dopamine hydrochloride amount, the surface CN content of the CDs progressively rises, introducing additional surface defects, these defects function as excitonic traps, narrowing the energy gap and increasing the photoluminescence quantum yield [29]. To realize efficient RTP emission, an efficient ISC process is required from S1 to Tn, and the ISC rate is in turn determined by the strong spin-orbit coupling. According to the El-Sayed rule, effective SOC is achieved by transitioning from a unilinear state with an electronic configuration such as 1(π, π*) or 1(n, π*) to triplet states such as 3(n, π*) or 3(π, π*), thus the ISC process between S1 and Tn with similar electronic configurations is suppressed [35]. The sp2 π-conjugated carbon structure of the CDs in CDSL-400 provides a large amount of (π, π*) transitions. The lone-pair electrons in the n-orbitals of the CDs' abundant surface functional groups form n-π* transitions with the π-orbitals of the aromatic carbon core, enabling efficient ISC and generating a sufficient population of triplet excitons [36]. The S1 and Tn can be regarded as a combination of the (n, π*) transitions (αn) and (π, π*) transitions (βπ) (α + β = 1), which provides an effective ISC and makes CDSL-400 with phosphorescence emission [37]. Atoms with n-orbital hybridization doped in CDs can effectively promote the ISC process of carbon dots-based RTP composites and improve the RTP efficiency. Thus, the introduction of nitrogen atoms in dopamine hydrochloride generates more charge transfer from the π-conjugated carbon core structure to the surface functional groups in CDSLX-400 (X = 5, 10, and 20), which enhances the share of αn (n, π*) transitions, accelerates the ISC transitions. Concurrently, the presence of unsaturated surface groups (C=O/C=N) increases the CDs' surface defect density, which can form localized electronic states that trap excitons, extend their lifetime, and suppress non-radiative transitions [35].

The effect of reaction temperature on the luminescence properties of CDSL10-T is further explored. CDSL10-T samples all present blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation and obvious afterglow phenomenon after turning off the light source. The afterglow color of CDSL10–300 is yellowish compared to the other samples (Fig. 3a-(a-III)). The afterglow times of CDSL10-T are 5 s, 6 s, 7 s, and 6 s, respectively, and the afterglow time first increases and then decreases upon raising reaction temperature. This is because that the reaction temperature is below the melting point of the molten salt (334 °C for KNO3) and the rigid salt shell is not completely formed. When the temperature exceeds 360 °C, the fully formed molten salt rigid layer outside the CDs protects the triplet excitons from quenching and promotes afterglow emission [38]. When temperature further goes above 400 °C, the phosphorescence time becomes shorter, which may stem from the limited thermal stability and lose of activity for surface functional groups of CDs [23].

Fluorescence spectrum, UV–vis spectrum, and phosphorescence spectrum are used to characterize the optical properties of CDSL10-T (Fig. 3 and Table S5). CDSL10-T sample exhibits excitation wavelength dependence caused by multiple functional groups on the surface of CDs ((Fig. 3b-(b-III.)), with a characteristic excitation wavelength of 350 nm corresponding to 403, 397, 399, and 399 nm, and FLQYs of 2.22, 7.80, 7.98, and 5.90 %, respectively (Table S5). The FLQY of CDSL10–300 is significantly lower than that of other samples, and its fluorescence spectrum is significantly broader. This may be attributed to that lignin feedstock is not completely carbonized at 300 °C, and there are some unreacted small molecule products and CDs with non-uniform sizes. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurs between these multi-luminescent centers, resulting in the decrease of the FLQY and the spectrum broadening [39]. With the temperature increasing, the fluorescence spectrum gradually narrows, which indicates that the degree of carbonization of CDs increases. Uniformly size distribution of CDs promotes the degree of aromatization of the carbon core structure, thus the FLQY also increases gradually. When the temperature exceeds 400 °C, the FLQY of CDs decreases due to the loss of activity of surface functional groups on the CDs. Therefore, selecting an appropriate carbonization temperature is important for regulating the luminescent properties of CDs. CDSL10-T has similar UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 3c-(c-III.)), and the two absorption peaks at 240 nm and 310 nm correspond to the π-π* conjugation of CC and the n-π* transition of C=O/C=N, respectively, indicating that there is no significant change in the structural composition of CDs formed at different temperatures. Phosphorescence emission spectra (Fig. 3d-(d-III)) show that the phosphorescent characteristic excitation wavelength of CDSL10-T is 350 nm, and the corresponding characteristic emission wavelengths are 523 nm, 501 nm, 503 nm, and 503 nm, respectively, with relative fluorescence red shifting and excitation wavelength-dependent behavior. High reaction temperature can increase the degree of carbonization of CDs. A high degree of dehydration leads to the generation of abundant strong electron-accepting functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which increases surface defects and enhances the optical transitions of luminescent centers related to the surface. This also enhances the intersystem crossing ability of triplet excitons. Additionally, high reaction temperatures accelerate the chemical reaction of molten salts, which is conducive to the formation of carbon dot shells [40]. The maximum emission peaks of the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of all samples show a significant blue shift with increasing temperature, due to CDs being more tightly embedded in a vibrationally confined environment, endowing an increase in the phosphorescence luminescence time.

Similarly, the UV–vis spectra of CDSL10-T can be divided into three absorption bands, as shown in Fig. 4a. With the increase of reaction temperature, the π–π* transition of CDSL10-T gradually increases, indicating the carbonization degree of CDs is significantly enhanced, and the carbon content of the sp2 structure increases. The n–π* transition absorption band of CDSL10-T first increases and then weakens, due to the increased temperature promotes intermolecular dehydration and to lead to increased CDs surface defects, which act as excitation traps to reduce the energy gap and increase the FLQY. Meanwhile the modulation of the singlet-triplet energy split caused by the n-π* transition contributes to more efficient capture of the triplet state and facilitates the ISC, thereby promoting phosphorescent emission [41]. In contrast, high temperatures affect the stability of CDs, which passivates some of the surface-active groups and leads to a decrease in fluorescence/phosphorescence lifetime. The fluorescence and phosphorescence decay curves of CDSL10-T can be fitted as a triexponential function (Fig. 4b-c), with fluorescence lifetimes of 6.25 ns, 8.78 ns, 10.07 ns, and 8.47 ns, respectively (Table S6), and phosphorescent lifetimes of 2.29 s, 2.31 s, 2.35 s, and 2.33 s, respectively (Table S7). As the temperature increases, the n–π* transition in CDSL10-T improves, resulting from an increase in the proportion of oxygen/nitrogen sp2 carbon and a decrease in ΔEST (Table S8) [42]. The above analysis yields the influence of temperature on the structure of CDSL10-T (Fig. 4 d). If the reaction temperature is too low, the molten salt shell is not fully formed, and the degree of carbonization of CDs is low, also the particle size is uneven, resulting in low FLQY and photoluminescence lifetime. As the temperature increases, the degree of carbonization of the CDs gradually improves, with enhanced homogeneous dots size and advanced aromatic carbon core structure. The abundance of oxygen and nitrogen atoms in the carbon‑nitrogen source precursor facilitates the formation of defects during carbonization, leading to increased surface defects of CDs, narrowing the energy gap, and enhancing the FLQY. Meanwhile, as the reaction temperature increases, the connection within inorganic salt shell becomes strong, stabilizing the triplet states. This generates slow quenching/non-radiation rates (Kq and Knr), which extends the phosphorescence lifetime. However, if the temperature goes too high, the stability of CDs decreases and the surface functional groups are passivated, which is not conducive to phosphorescence emission. Therefore, 400 °C is determined to be the most suitable reaction temperature.

In order to further explore the luminescence mechanism of composites and the influence of reaction temperature on the shell, the structural composition of CDSLX-T is characterized. The compositions of the CDSLX-T salt shells are analyzed using XRD patterns, and the peaks at 19°, 23°, 29°, 33°, 34°, 42°, and 47° belong to the orthorhombic crystal system KNO3(JCPDS#04-009-3532), peaks at 28°, 40°, 55°, and 66° belong to the cubic crystal system KCl(JCPDS#01-076- 3362), and peaks at 24°, 27°, 30°, and 44° belong to Mg3(PO4)2 in the monoclinic crystal system (JCPDS#00-033-0876), while peaks at 32°, 38°, and 73° belong to MgO in the cubic crystal system (JCPDS#04-006-0699). This indicates that the salt shell is primarily composed of four main components: KNO3, KCl, Mg3(PO4)2, and MgO (Fig. 5a) [43,44]. The XRD spectra (Fig. S2) of CDSLX-400 show a weak broad diffraction peak at 20–25°, which is a typical amorphous carbon peak and it suggests that their carbon cores consist of amorphous carbon, while the characteristic peaks of the salt shells do not change significantly, implying that the addition of different ratios of nitrogen sources does not affect the formation of the salt shell structure of the sample [29]. Fig. 5b shows the XRD patterns of CDSL10-T. The shell of CDSL10–300 consists of KNO3 in the orthorhombic crystal system, KCl in the cubic system, and MgCl2 in the orthorhombic system (JCPDS#01–070-2748). When the reaction temperature exceeds 300 °C, the shell of all samples consists of KNO3, KCl, Mg3(PO4)2, and MgO, indicating that the formation of MgO and Mg3(PO4)2 requires a temperature above 300 °C. Compared with the samples generated at low temperature, the characteristic peak intensity of CDSL10–450 samples is significantly enhanced, indicating that CDSL10–450 salt shell has the highest degree of crystallization. However, its phosphorescent lifetime is slightly lower than that of CDSL10–400, indicating that the composition of the CDs structure formed at different temperatures is also a determining factor affecting the luminescence lifetime.

Investigating the phase transition process of molten salts at different temperatures through in situ XRD reveals the formation process of the shell. The raw materials are freeze-drying according to the optimal ratio and then subjected to in situ XRD (Fig. 5c). The main composition of the mixture at room temperature (25 °C) is KNO3 in the orthorhombic crystal system, MgCl2 in the orthorhombic crystal system, and KH2PO4 in the tetragonal crystal system (JCPDS #00-035-0807). When the temperature rises to 95 °C, the peaks of KNO3, MgCl2, and KH2PO4 begin to weaken. As the temperature further increases to 145 °C, the peaks of MgCl2 and KH2PO4 disappear, and KCl of the cubic crystal system and the Mg(H2PO4)2 (JCPDS#00–040-0066) in tetragonal crystal system start to form [45]. This may be ascribed to some MgCl2 and KH2PO4 turn into Mg(H2PO4)2 as follows:
(3)

At the temperature, the KNO3 crystalline changes from orthorhombic crystal to rhombic crystal (JCPDS#01-076-1692). As the temperature continues to increase, the KCl peaks of the cubic crystal system and the KNO3 diffraction peaks of the rhombic crystal system are shifted to the left. This is because most materials have a positive thermal expansion coefficient, meaning they expand with increasing temperature, which is reflected in the XRD pattern as a leftward shift of the diffraction peaks. At the same time, the peaks of Mg(H2PO4)2 and KNO3 gradually weaken. When the temperature reaches 360 °C, the peaks of Mg(H2PO4)2 and KNO3 disappear, indicating that they have reached the molten state, and only KCl in the cubic crystal system is detected. Temperatures in the range of 360–450 °C give the same assay results as at 360 °C, indicating that all salts except KCl are in a molten state. During the cooling down process, in addition to KCl in the cubic crystal system, Mg3(PO4)2 in monoclinic crystal system (JCPDS#00-033-0876), MgO in cubic crystal system (JCPDS#04-006-0699), and KNO3 in orthorhombic crystal system are detected as a result of recrystallization. In MS, the aggregation of metal ions is suppressed, allowing metal ions to move freely within the molten salt. Additionally, molten KNO3 can be viewed both as a reaction medium and base, providing oxide ions (O2−). It is speculated that the formation of MgO and Mg3(PO4)2 benefits from the solubility of metal ions in the molten salt [17].

To further investigate which part of the molten salt plays a key role in phosphorescence emission, a series of comparative experiments are conducted, and no phosphorescence phenomenon is observed in the products obtained without the addition of MgCl2, KNO3, and KH2PO4 during the preparation of the CDLS10–400. The results point out that synergistic interactions among KNO3, KCl, Mg3(PO4)2, and MgO structures in the shell of CDLS10–400 promote the phosphorescence emission from CDs. Sample without KH2PO4 addition shows partial fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, but still no phosphorescence (Fig. S3a). The Raman spectra of the comparison samples are examined, and multiple absorption peaks located at 710, 1038, and 1359 cm−1 are attributed to KNO3, peaks at 848, 991, and 1078 cm−1 are attributed to phosphate, peaks at 372 and 1674 cm−1 are attributed to MgCl2, and peaks at 1295 and 1580 cm−1 correspond to the D-band and G-band, respectively (Fig. S3b) [24,46,47]. The D-band represents disorder and lattice defects in the graphene structure, and the G-band represents a highly ordered graphite structure. Raman spectroscopy indicates that the absence of any of the salts prevents the formation of a complete shell. The ID/IG of pure CDs (CDLS10–400 without MgCl2, KNO3, and KH2PO4) and CDLS10–400 are 0.35 and 0.44, respectively, indicating that the CDs contain abundant aromatic structural domains with a high degree of graphitization originating from the abundant aromatic structures present in the precursor sodium lignosulfonate. Pure CDs (CDLS10–400 without MgCl2, KNO3, and KH2PO4) are dispersed in ethanol, and under 365 nm UV irradiation, CDs show a blue fluorescence with a color similar to that of CDLS10–400, and there is no phosphorescence when the light source is turned off (Fig. S4). It is shown that sodium lignosulfonate can also form CDs with blue fluorescence after calcination. The CDs in the aggregated state will produce non-radiative transitions, the CDs with fluorescence characteristics in the solution phase are fluorescently quenched in the aggregate state [37]. Thus the fluorescence phenomenon cannot be observed in the powder state due to the interaction between particles. In addition, CDLS20–360-Al is prepared by replacing MgCl2 with AlCl3, and the afterglow time is only 2 s, which is significantly lower than that of CDLS20–360 of about 6 s (Fig. S5), suggesting that Mg ions play an important role in promoting the process of long-lived phosphorescence emission from CDs. The XRD patterns of CDSL20–360 reveal the shell composes of KNO3, KCl, Mg3(PO4)2, and MgO, whereas CDSL20–360-Al exhibits only KNO3 and KCl, due to the higher temperature requirements for aluminum salt formation (Fig. S6). Additionally, the reduced peak intensity of KNO3 in CDSL20–360-Al suggests diminished crystallinity in its salt shell. The difference in phosphorescence lifetime mainly originates from the difference in shell structure, in which Mg plays an important role in promoting the long-lived phosphorescence emission of CDs [48]. Mg exists as Mg3(PO4)2 and MgO within an inorganic salt shell, and the CDs are connected to the magnesium salt by the C-Mg covalent bond, thereby stabilizing the triple exciton, inhibiting the energy dissipation pathway, and prolonging the phosphorescence time [49]. On other hand, the luminescent groups (C=O/C=N) on the surface of the CDs can be seen as electric dipoles, whose emission results from the recombination of photogenerated charges at the center of the defect. High charge density Mg ions enhance the dipoles in the CDs and promote the ISC process, which in turn increases the efficiency of RTP emission, resulting in a longer phosphorescence lifetime [43].

Based on the above analysis, the formation of CDSL10–400 shell is proposed: KNO3, phosphate, and magnesium salts with high charge density are selected as shell materials. in the initial low-temperature reaction stage, a portion of the MgO and KH2PO4 reacts to produce Mg(H2PO4)2 and KCl. Concurrently, as the temperature rises, KNO3 gradually transforms into a molten salt medium, which accelerates the process of converting inorganic salts such as MgCl2 and phosphate into liquid state. When the temperature reaches 360 °C, all salts other than KCl transition to molten phases [45]. Simultaneously, oxygen-containing functional groups in lignin, including hydroxyl and methoxy groups, break off to form lignin fragments [50]. With prolonged reaction time, these lignin fragments are converted through dehydration, polymerization, and condensation reactions into primary aromatic clusters [34]. Subsequently, under high temperature and long reaction time conditions, these primary aromatic clusters undergo rapid π-π bond accumulation, a sp2 hybridization increase, and aromatic polymerization [51]. Aromatic clusters and dopamine hydrochloride are carbonized in situ into carbon cores composed primarily of sp2-hybridized carbon, while some oxygen-containing groups (e.g., -COOH, -OH) in the lignin precursor remain on the surface of the carbon core, forming surface defects and functionalized sites that contribute to the formation of CDs [33]. Under high-temperature conditions, the degree of carbonization of the CDs gradually increases, leading to improved size homogeneity and a more ordered aromatic carbon core structure [52]. High reaction temperature can increase the degree of carbonization of CDs. The high degree of dehydration leads to the generation of abundant strong electron-accepting functional groups on the CDs' surface, which increases surface defects and enhances the optical transitions of luminescent centers related to the surface [53]. This also enhances the intersystem crossing ability of triplet excitons. In the constant temperature reaction stage, molten potassium nitrate can provide O2− as the reaction medium, and metal Mg ions and phosphate ions move freely in the molten salt, leading to the formation of MgO and Mg3(PO4)2 [17]. During the cooling process, MgO, Mg3(PO4)2, KCl, and recrystallized KNO3 form the rigid salt shell. This stable inorganic shell effectively immobilizes the CDs through spatial confinement, thereby suppressing intramolecular vibrations, stabilizing their triplet excited states, and reducing non-radiative decay and quenching processes [40].

TEM is used to analyze the microstructure and particle size distribution of CDs in CDSL10–400 composites. Results show that CDs are well dispersed in a near-spherical shape with an average particle size of 2.47 nm (Fig. 5d-e) and are uniformly dispersed in a salt matrix, which further confirms the successful construction of carbon dot composites with salt shell structure. The HRTEM images (Fig. 5f) show that CDs have a well-resolved lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which is attributed to the (100) plane of the graphitized carbon core, indicating that CDs are highly graphitized and that there is an abundant π-conjugated structure in the carbon cores [9].

XPS analysis is conducted on the functional groups and structural composition of CDSL10–400. The samples are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, magnesium, chlorine, and potassium with contents of 8.15 %, 10.14 %, 44.75 %, 2.68 %, 17.08 %, 6.47 %, and 10.91 %, respectively (Fig. 6a). The C 1s spectra (Fig. 6b) binding energies at 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.6 eV represent C-C/C=C, CN, CO, and C=O/C=N functional groups, respectively, suggesting that the CDs have a sp2/sp3 conjugated carbon core structure modified by sp3 hybridized carbon groups [25]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 6c) can be deconvoluted into five peaks, which are pyridine nitrogen (C-N=C, N-6, ∼398.3 eV), pyrrolic nitrogen (C-N-C, N-5, ∼399.9 eV), graphitic nitrogen (N-(C)3, N-Q, ∼401.4 eV), oxidized nitrogen (C-N-O, N-X, ∼403.5 eV), and KNO3 (407.1 eV). This demonstrates that nitrogen atoms are successfully doped into the CDs. The doped nitrogen atoms tend to exist in a stable conjugated structure of six-membered rings, primarily in the form of pyridine nitrogen and oxidized nitrogen, which can effectively enhance the fluorescence efficiency and intensity of the CDs. Graphitic nitrogen can maintain the sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms and provide π-delocalized electrons. This increases the radiative processes in the aromatic domain. Additionally, nitrogen-related functional groups can enhance electron cloud densities and increase the SOC constant, thereby promoting the ISC process [42]. The peaks at 533.3 eV, 532.5 eV, 531.5 eV, and 530.8 eV in the O 1s spectra (Fig. 6d) represent CO, PO, CO, and PO functional groups, respectively, suggesting an abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs. It illustrates that the P–O/P=O groups are the primary oxygen-containing groups with a small amount of C-O/C=O groups [54]. It The peak at 133.5 eV in the P 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 6e) can be attributed to P-O/P=O [46]. The Mg 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 6f) can be fitted to a peak at 1304.86 eV, which belongs to MgO, and the XPS spectrum of Mg 2p (Fig. 6g) can be fitted to two peaks, centered at 50.15 eV and 48.9 eV, respectively, belonging to MgO and MgC, respectively [43]. The XPS spectrum of Cl 2p (Fig. 6h) is deconvoluted into two peaks for Cl- (198.59 eV) and CCl (200.15 eV) [44]. XPS spectra of K 2p (Fig. 6i) indicates the presence of KCl (292.47 eV), KNO3 (292.91 eV), and CK (295.53 eV) in CDSL10–400 [55]. The analysis further proves that the shell is composed of MgO, KNO3, KCl, and phosphate, which is consistent with the XRD conclusion. It is also shown that the CDs are successfully embedded in the inorganic salt shell, and the CDs are stably connected to the shell through the form of CK, CCl, and CMg covalent bonds.

XPS spectrum analysis shows that the CDs in CDSL10–400 consist of graphitized conjugated carbon cores as well as carbon shells containing oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. The presence of N- and O-groups promotes the conjugation of electrons to the aromatic structure, resulting in fluorescence [6]. The introduction of nitrogen atoms enables new CN functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which enhances the n-π* transitions and promotes a strong SOC, reducing the energy gap between singlet and triplet states and accelerating the ISC process, thereby achieving efficient RTP emission [25]. Fig. 6j-k show the schematic structure and luminescence mechanism of CDSL-400 and CDSL10–400. The aromatic carbonyl functional group of CDs in CDSL-400 can promote the singlet to triplet ISC process, but the ISC process is less efficient due to the inherently localized π-π* transitions dominating singlet and triplet states [26]. By doping strategy with heteroatoms, nitrogen atoms as electron donors and unpaired electrons can enhance the optical properties of CDs. A large number of CN functional groups rich in n-π* transitions are introduced into the CDs, increasing the proportion of αn(n, π*) transitions, and promoting the excited energy level of the triplet states, thereby reducing the energy gap ΔEST [25]. Concurrently, the doped N atoms exist in the CDs in a stable six-membered-ring conjugated structure, which effectively improves the fluorescence efficiency and optical lifetime of the material [21]. CDs are successfully embedded in the salt matrix shell, with covalent bonds (CK, CCl, and CMg) connecting the CDs and the salt shell. The heteroatoms in the inorganic salt matrix form new functional groups (CK, CCl, and CMg) with CDs, and these functional groups also create lone pairs of electrons, and promote the transition of n-π* in the system [34]. The spatial constraints of the inorganic salt matrix and the multiple constraints of the covalent bond between the CDs and the salt shell reduce the vibration and rotation of CDs, stabilize the triplet excitons, and reduce the energy dissipation path of the generated triplet excitons so that CDSL10–400 has a stable and long-life phosphorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Universality of the molten salt method
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the universality of the molten salt method for constructing biomass-based phosphorescent carbon materials, chitosan, black liquor lignin, and microcrystalline cellulose are used as carbon sources, respectively, and the composites are constructed by the above molten salt method. The phosphorescent properties of the composites constructed by different carbon sources are explored in comparison with those of CDSL-360 and CDSL10–360. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, CDC-360, CDBL-360, CDM-360, CDSL-360, and CDSL10–360 emit blue fluorescence. After turning off the light source, the yellow-green afterglow phenomenon is observed for 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 s, respectively (Fig. S7), indicating that the molten salt method proposed in this experiment is suitable for most biomass carbon sources to prepare phosphorescent CDs. Among them, CDSL-360 and CDSL10–360 have the longest luminescence time, indicating that the structure of the raw materials has a direct effect on the phosphorescent lifetime. Compared with the straight-chain structure of polysaccharides (such as cellulose and chitosan), lignin contains abundant sp2-hybrid benzene ring structures making it easier to form CDs with highly graphitized carbon cores under the same reaction conditions, showing highly stable optical properties. Black liquor is a by-product of the industrial papermaking process, containing rich lignin and a small amount of cellulose and hemicellulose. The lignin in its component is highly aggregated, and its composition and structure are complex, with a wide range of molecular weight distribution, resulting in low reactivity and unstable performance [19]. Compared to purified lignin, it is not easy to prepare CDs from black liquor. Sodium lignosulfonate combines rich benzene ring structure and functional groups of lignin with good water solubility. The syringyl structural units within it are responsible for the phosphorescent emission, making it an ideal precursor for the preparation of CDs [56]. Thus, compared to chitosan, microcrystalline cellulose, and black liquor lignin as the carbon sources present the longest phosphorescence luminescence time.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the different carbon source samples all show a typical excitation wavelength dependence, with little difference in the corresponding characteristic emission wavelengths (Fig. S8 a-(a-III) and Table S9). The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of CDC-360, CDBL-360, CDM-360, CDSL-360, and CDSL10–360 are 75 nm, 107 nm, 95 nm, 62 nm, and 58 nm, respectively, with the wider FWHM indicating lower color purity and uneven particle size distribution. Molecular precursors with large conjugated structures are more conducive to the preparation of CDs with uniform structure and narrow FWHM. Therefore, the CDs with sodium lignosulfonate as the carbon source have a narrower FWHM [57]. while unpurified black liquor lignin contains components such as polysaccharide precipitates, ash, and salts, resulting in uneven particle size distribution and relatively wide FWHM. The samples in the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Fig. S8 b-(b-III) all have two absorption peaks near 240 nm and 300 nm, which originate from the π-π* conjugated aromatic system of CC and the n-π* transitions of C=O/C=N, respectively. Meanwhile, CDBL-360 has a typical B absorption band at 265 nm, which suggests that there is a separate aromatic structure [52]. The phosphorescence spectra of different carbon source samples exhibited less obvious excitation wavelength dependence and relative fluorescence redshift (Fig. S8 c-(c-III)). The three absorption bands in the UV spectra (Fig. S9) show that CDSL10–360 has higher π-π* transitions, n-π* transitions, and multiple surface-defective states. The higher n-π* transitions originate from the abundant C=O/C=N functional groups in CDSL10–360, which can promote RTP emission and make CDSL10–360 with excellent optical properties compared with other carbon source samples. The ΔEST of CDC-360, CDBL-360, CDM-360, and CDSL-360 are 0.65 eV, 0.77 eV, 0.66 eV, and 0.62 eV, respectively, according to the energy level formula (Table S10). CDSL-360 has the smallest ΔEST of 0.62 eV. The smaller the ΔEST, the more favorable it is for the ISC process and the easier it is to emit phosphorescence.

The microstructures and particle size distributions of the three carbon sources are analyzed by TEM (Fig. S10). Results show that the CDs obtained from different carbon sources are uniformly distributed in the inorganic salt matrix, which further confirming the universality of the molten salt method. The average particle sizes of CDC-360, CDBL-360, and CDM-360 are 2.44 nm, 2.65 nm, and 2.39 nm, respectively. From the HRTEM images, all CDs have lattice stripe with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which is attributed to the (100) plane of the graphitized carbon cores [9]. CDBL-360 has a wider particle size distribution and a larger average particle size due to the complexity of the components in the black liquor, which results in a wider full width at half maximum of the fluorescence spectra and lower luminescence color purity and FLQY [11]. Therefore, although the main component in the carbon source of CDBL-360 is lignin, its optical lifetime is shorter than that of CDSL10–400. The functional groups and structural composition of CDC-360, CDBL-360, and CDM-360 are analyzed by XPS (Fig. S11). The CDs in CDC-360, CDBL-360, and CDM-360 consist of C=C/C-C, CO, and CO, indicating that the CDs contain complex aromatic structures and oxygen-containing functional groups, whereas CDC-360 also contains C=N/C-N functional groups due to the presence of nitrogen atoms. These oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups containing lone-pair electrons contribute to the formation of n-π* transitions and promote triple exciton ISC generation [36]. As shown in Table S11, the gradual increase in the C=C/C-C ratios of CDC-360, CDBL-360, CDM-360, and CDSL10–400 indicates that CDSL10–400 possesses a higher degree of graphitization and conjugated structures, providing a stable luminescent core. This enhancement originates from the abundant aromatic structures in lignin retained within the CDs. In contrast, CDC-360, CDBL-360, and CDM-360 lack aromatic conjugation systems, which limits their phosphorescence efficiency [58]. The gradual increase of the C=O/C-O ratios of CDC-360, CDBL-360, CDM-360 and CDSL10–400 indicate that CDSL10–400 has more oxygen-containing functional groups. These groups improve the SOC efficiency, generate a large number of triplet excitons, and stabilize phosphorescence emission [26]. Furthermore, covalent bonding between CDs and the inorganic salt matrix (C–K/C–Cl/C–Mg) provide stabilization for triplet excitons, enabling sustained phosphorescence emission. TEM and XPS demonstrate that the differences in phosphorescence time of the samples with different carbon sources originate from the differences in CDs' structure. Compared with the straight-chain structures of cellulose and chitosan, lignin contains abundant benzene ring structures, which are more likely to form CDs with highly graphitized carbon cores under the same reaction conditions and exhibit highly stable optical properties [59]. Additionally, lignin's oxygen-containing functional groups promote surface state formation in CDs, thereby enhancing ISC to produce triplet excitons [25]. The lignin in the black liquor is highly aggregated and has low reactivity, resulting in uneven size distribution of CDs and fewer oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface in CDBL-360 [19]. Consequently, CDSL10–400 has the longest phosphorescence time compared to CDC-360, CDBL-360 and CDM-360.

Most of the current CDs-based phosphorescent materials emit phosphorescence only in the solid state. This is due to the presence of dissolved oxygen in the solvent, solvent-assisted relaxation, and strong intermolecular interactions that can be interrupted and disrupted. Intense molecular motion leads to significant non-radiative dissipation, resulting in the quenching of phosphorescence in CDs, thereby hindering the application of RTP materials in the liquid phase [14]. The above-mentioned CDSLX-400 is dispersed in aqueous solution to form a homogeneous aqueous solution. CDSLX-400-L exhibits phosphorescence (Fig. S12), and CDSL10–400-L exhibits a 3 s green afterglow under 365 nm wavelength UV light. From the above phenomenon, it can be concluded that there are stable covalent bonds between the salt shell and the CDs, which can effectively suppress intramolecular motion and stabilize the triplet states. At the same time, the rigid salt shell isolates the CDs from the dissolved oxygen in the aqueous solution, enabling phosphorescent emission of the CDs in the solution. However, the afterglow time of CDSLX-400-L is shorter than that of CDSL10–400 solid powder (Fig. 7a). When the CDSLX-400 are dispersed in water, some of the soluble salts in the rigid shell are dissolved by water, and the vibration and rotation of the CDs increase, resulting in an accelerated non-radiative transition rate and a decrease in phosphorescent lifetime [60].

The optical properties of CDSL10–400-L are characterized. CDSL10–400-L has an excitation wavelength dependence with a characteristic excitation wavelength of 340 nm, corresponding to a characteristic emission wavelength of 388 nm for blue luminescence region of the spectral band (Fig. 7b). CDSL10–400-L and CDSL10–400 have similar UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 7c), and the two typical absorption peaks at 240 and 300 nm correspond to the π-π* conjugation of aromatic CC and the n-π* transition of C=O/C=N, respectively, indicating that the structural composition of CDs has not changed. The phosphorescence spectrum (Fig. 7d) shows that the characteristic excitation wavelength for phosphorescence of CDSL10–400-L is 330 nm, and the corresponding characteristic emission wavelength is 475 nm, which exhibits an insignificant excitation wavelength dependence. The ΔEST is calculated to be 0.59 eV according to eq. 2 (Table S12). Compared to the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDSL10–400, the fluorescence and phosphorescence peaks of CDSL10–400-L are significantly blue-shifted, the phosphorescence color is blue-shifted from a yellow-green color to a green color, and the slight blue-shift of the emission wavelengths between the solid and solution samples could be caused by solvent effects [54]. The UV–vis spectra show that the luminescent center of the CDs is unchanged, indicating that water mainly causes damage to part of the shell of CDSL10–400 and affects its optical properties. When CDSL10–400 is dispersed in water, some soluble salts attach to the surface or gaps of the salt shell dissolve due to intermolecular forces, causing the shell structure of the CDs to become loose, but the rigid covalent bonds remain. The stable covalent bond provides a rigid shell for the CDs to remain stable in water, avoiding the quenching effect of water and dissolved oxygen molecules in the water on the CDs, thus achieving the phosphorescence emission of CDs in water. Phosphorescent phenomena can be observed in samples from different carbon sources dispersed in water, indicating that the molten salt method is universal and suitable for most biomass carbon materials to prepare structurally stable room-temperature phosphorescent materials (Fig. S13).

Based on the aforementioned results, the luminescence mechanisms of CDSL10–400 and CDSL10–400-L are proposed (Fig. 7e). During the formation of CDs, the benzene ring structure inherent in lignin is retained in the carbon nucleus, and the oxygen-rich group is modified on the surface of the carbon shell. When the CDs are irradiated with UV light, the unsaturated π-electrons and lone-pair electrons in CDSL10–400 are excited, and the electrons transit from S0 to Sn. The functional groups on the surface of the CDs act as electron traps and capture the electrons, which subsequently return from S1 to S0 via radiative transfer and emit blue fluorescence [50]. The abundant (π, π*) transitions from carbon-dot sp2 π conjugated carbon structure and the small number of (n, π*) transitions from aromatic carbonyls on the surface provide different electronic configurations for S1 and Tn, which facilitates efficient ISC and makes it easy for excited state electrons to travel from S1 to Tn [26]. Heteroatomic nitrogen brings additional CN for the CDs to promote the share of (n, π*) transitions in S1 and Tn. This allows the px and py orbitals to overlap efficiently under the action of the orbital angular momentum operator Lz, enhancing SOC, benefiting effective ISC process to populate triplet excitons. The excitons returning to S0 through radiative transitions leads to phosphorescence [61]. Through the covalent bonding between the CDs and the salt shell and the spatial restriction of the inorganic salt shell, the quenching of the triplet excitons is avoided, and the efficient long-lived yellow-green afterglow emission of CDSL10–400 is achieved [32]. The covalent bonds existing between the shell and the CDs can effectively suppress the intramolecular vibrations and rotations of the CDs and reduce the nonradiative attenuation, thus stabilizing the triplet states, which is the first confinement effect. The inorganic salt shell builds a rigid environment for the CDs, isolating the CDs from the phosphorescence quenching factors in the external environment, and reducing the quenching/nonradiative rate of the triplet excitons in the CDs, which is the second confinement effect [14]. When CDSL10–400 is dispersed in water, the covalent bond between CDs and the shell still exists stably, suppressing the rotation and vibration of the luminescent groups on the surface of CDs in water. The insoluble rigid shell protects CDs from water and the dissolved oxygen in the water, thus this dual-confinement enables CDSL10–400 to realize stable RTP emission even in water. The soluble inorganic salts KNO3 and KCl in the salt shells are attached to the surface of the salt shells or in the interstitial spaces by intermolecular forces, which are partially destroyed in the liquid. When the oxygen molecules in the water collide with CDs, the excited triplet state of the CDs transfers the energy to the oxygen molecules (the dissolved oxygen in aqueous solution with triplet state), causing the oxygen to get electrons to become a single line state. This leads to the deactivation of its triplet excited state and the quenching of the phosphorescence emission, resulting in a shorter phosphorescent lifetime of CDSL10–400-L compared to the solid state [17].

Based on the stable phosphorescent properties and different phosphorescent lifetimes of the carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent material CDSLX-T, it can be used as an ideal time-dependent anti-counterfeiting material. Fig. 8a presents different afterglow times of the samples CDSL10–400, CDSL10–360, and CDSL10–300. After turning off the UV light, a simple design is created using these three materials to form a code: “QUST”. Under 365 nm UV light, “QUST” emits blue fluorescence. A complete yellow “QUST” can be observed immediately after turning off the UV light. After 2 s, “QST” can be seen, and after 4 s, only the safety code “Q” is visible. This demonstrates its broad application prospects in the field of time-resolved anti-counterfeiting. CDSLX-T can also be used for information encryption. Fig. 8b shows the application of CDSLX-T in the area of message encryption, as shown in the Video S1, where different information can be observed by simply removing the UV excitation light source. By combining CDSL10–400 and CDSL10–300, the letters “888” (error information) can be observed under 254 nm UV light. After turning off the UV light, the “SOS” signal (correct message) lasting 3 s is observed. After removing the UV light, the weaker phosphorescent emission of CDSL10–300 is difficult to observe under environmental light interference. Due to the stronger phosphorescence emission of CDSL10–400, the correct message “SOS” can be clearly observed for about 3 s even under the interference of environmental light (Video S2 and fig. S14). When the phosphorescence emission from the CDSL10–400 is finished, the non-emitting error message “888” is again observed due to environmental light interference. The above results demonstrate that the composite material can be observed to apply information encryption to the CDSLX-T even under the interference of external light. The fingerprint recognition function of CDSL10–400 is investigated by imprinting fingerprints on glass. Under 254 nm UV light, the core and bifurcation of the fingerprints are observed, and the fingerprint is still visible after 10 days (Fig. 8c), which demonstrates that CDSL10–400 can be effectively used in areas such as personal identity verification. The fingerprint recognition function of CDSL10–400 is investigated by imprinting fingerprints on glass. The fingerprints can be clearly observed under daylight and under 254 nm UV light (Fig. 8c and Fig. S15). After removing the UV light, clear fingerprints remain visible due to the stable phosphorescent properties of CDSL10–400 (Video S3). To assess thermal stability, the fingerprints on the glass plate are placed in the environment of −4 °C and 40 °C for 4 h. Phosphorescent emission of fingerprints can still be clearly observed at the corresponding temperatures, which proves the stability of CDSL10–400 in fingerprint identification (Fig. S15). In order to demonstrate the reproducibility of the CDSL10–400 for fingerprint security applications under real conditions, two other different substrates (plastic and aluminum foil) are chosen. Fig. S16, Video S4, and Video S5 show clear fingerprint images developed with CDSL10–400 under daylight and 254 nm UV light. The fingerprints remain visible after UV light removal, attributed to the persistent phosphorescence of CDSL10–400. The above results prove the excellent effect of CDSL10–400 in developing fingerprints on different substrates, indicating its broad application prospect in the field of fingerprint anti-counterfeiting. Given the CDSL10–400-L can still maintain stable phosphorescence emission in liquids, CDSL10–400 is dispersed in ethanol solvent to explore its application for information anti-counterfeiting in liquids. A safety ink is prepared by dispersing CDSL10–400 in ethanol to write “QUST” on a plastic plate. Under 254 nm UV light, the letters show blue fluorescence, it is difficult to distinguish the letters due to the interference of background light. After turning off UV light, the letters emit blue phosphorescence and the “QUST” can be easily distinguished by the naked eye (Fig. 8d). Due to its excellent stability in liquids, CDSL10–400 can be used as an advanced security ink for information encryption.

We develop a core-shell carbon dot composite (CDSLX-T) with stable room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) through a one-step molten salt strategy using sodium lignosulfonate and inorganic salts. The lignin-derived carbon cores, enriched with aromatic carbonyl and nitrogen-doped D-π-A configurations, synergistically enhance intersystem crossing by promoting n → π* transitions and spin-orbit coupling. High-temperature processing simultaneously achieves carbon core graphitization and rigid crystalline salt shell formation (MgO/Mg3(PO4)2/KCl), where covalent C-K/C-Cl/C-Mg bonds and spatial confinement collaboratively suppress non-radiative decay. The optimal CDSL10–400 exhibits dual emission with a fluorescence lifetime of 10.07 ns and phosphorescence lasting 2.35 s, maintaining stability in aqueous environments due to its tightly anchored 2.47 nm carbon dots. In situ XRD reveals the staged salt matrix evolution, where molten KNO3 mediates Mg oxide/phosphate crystallization while KCl recrystallizes during cooling. Leveraging time-resolved luminescence and environmental robustness, the composites demonstrate versatile applications in fingerprint visualization, anti-counterfeiting inks, and multilevel information encryption. This work not only establishes a universal molten salt platform for converting biomass into advanced optical materials but also paves the way for sustainable photonic systems by bridging renewable feedstocks with high-performance emission engineering. Future efforts will focus on expanding the biomass precursor library and optimizing interfacial dynamics for industrial-scale production.
